structured_choices.data_source: Electronic health records (EHR) from a single regional healthcare system spanning five years (Jan 2019–Dec 2023), approximately 100,000 patient records with complete demographic, symptom, diagnosis, treatment, lab result, and clinical note data. Data extracted at the patient-encounter level with longitudinal follow-up outcomes.
structured_choices.label_definition: Disease progression measured as the annualized rate of change in a validated clinical severity index (e.g., composite score of lab markers, functional status, hospitalization frequency, or specialist referrals) over a 12-month follow-up window. Patients are stratified into three progression categories: stable (annual change < 10th percentile), moderate (10th–90th percentile), and rapid (> 90th percentile). Primary outcome is modeled as continuous (annualized severity change); secondary stratified evaluation uses the three-category classification.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split with stratification by disease type and baseline severity. Training set: patients with index encounter in Jan 2019–Dec 2020 (n ≈ 45,000). Validation set: index encounter in Jan 2021–Jun 2021 (n ≈ 20,000). Test set: index encounter in Jul 2021–Dec 2021 (n ≈ 15,000). Holdout site-based check: 10% of test set (n ≈ 1,500) drawn from a second healthcare facility within the system to assess generalization across institutional practice variation. Rationale: temporal split prevents information leakage from future visits into training. Stratification ensures each split maintains the distribution of disease type and baseline severity, reducing variance in outcome distributions and enabling fair comparison of model behavior across patient subgroups.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing and feature engineering transformers are fit exclusively on the training set. Scaling (standardization of continuous features), imputation (median for continuous, mode for categorical), and temporal feature encoding are learned from training data only. Validation and test sets are transformed using the fitted training-set parameters. This prevents data leakage and ensures the model is evaluated on truly unseen distribution shifts.
structured_choices.features: Raw inputs: (1) Free-text clinical notes from the prior 6–12 months (preprocessed via a pretrained clinical BERT tokenizer and truncated to 512 tokens); (2) Structured symptom and diagnosis codes (ICD-10) encoded as one-hot vectors; (3) Demographic features: age, sex, race/ethnicity, zip code–derived socioeconomic status (SES) quintile; (4) Baseline health data: comorbidity count, baseline lab values (creatinine, HbA1c, etc.), and prior medication classes; (5) Temporal features: time since last visit, visit frequency (encounters per year), days from index date. Derived features: (6) interaction terms between key comorbidities and baseline severity; (7) rolling statistics (mean, std dev) of lab values over preceding 12 months; (8) indicator variables for missing lab values (to capture missing-not-at-random patterns). All continuous features are centered and scaled post-imputation. Categorical features are one-hot encoded.
structured_choices.model: Transformer-based sequence model: A pretrained clinical BERT encoder processes the concatenated sequence of clinical note tokens and timestamped structured events (symptoms, diagnoses, labs, medications) encoded as special tokens with positional embeddings reflecting visit timing. The encoder output (final [CLS] token) is concatenated with demographic and baseline health features, then passed through a 2-layer feedforward network (256 → 128 → 1) to predict continuous disease progression. Model is trained end-to-end with a Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss for the primary regression task, with L2 regularization (λ = 0.001) to prevent overfitting.
structured_choices.baseline: Traditional regression baseline: Multivariable linear regression with elastic net regularization (α = 0.5, mixing L1 and L2 penalties). The baseline uses the same feature set as the transformer: structured symptoms, diagnoses, demographics, baseline health, and temporal features, but excludes raw clinical note text (instead using aggregated summary statistics from notes if available, e.g., presence of keywords). The baseline is tuned equivalently to the transformer: hyperparameters are selected via 5-fold cross-validation on the training set using the same primary metric (see below). This ensures fair comparison: both models receive the same preprocessing, the same structured features, and equivalent effort devoted to hyperparameter optimization. The baseline is not given a reduced feature set or less tuning effort.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) of predicted disease progression (annualized severity change) on the test set, reported at the whole-cohort level and stratified by baseline severity tertile and disease type. RMSE is chosen because (1) the outcome is continuous, (2) RMSE penalizes large errors more heavily, which is clinically relevant—large prediction errors lead to over- or under-treatment, and (3) RMSE is in units interpretable to clinicians (annualized severity points). Secondary threshold-based metric: If predictions are to inform treatment decisions (binary: intensify vs. maintain), the model's area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC) is computed at the threshold corresponding to the 75th percentile of predicted progression.
secondary_metrics: Mean Absolute Error (MAE) on the test set — clinically interpretable absolute error, less sensitive to outliers than RMSE.
Calibration plots (predicted vs. observed progression) stratified by prediction decile — ensures model uncertainty is well-calibrated; poor calibration suggests systematic bias.
Spearman correlation between predicted and observed progression — rank correlation robust to outliers.
Sensitivity and specificity for rapid progression detection (top 10% of progression outcomes) at the threshold chosen via validation set — directly relevant if model is used for triage.
Subgroup performance: RMSE, MAE, and correlation for each disease type, baseline severity tertile, SES quintile, and age group — flags potential disparities and model brittleness.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning is performed via 5-fold stratified cross-validation on the training set only, stratified by disease type and baseline severity. The primary metric (RMSE) is minimized on cross-validation folds. The validation set (index encounter Jul 2021–Jun 2021) is used once to select the final model from the top 3 candidate configurations identified by cross-validation and to set any threshold parameters (e.g., for the rapid-progression detection secondary metric). The test set is held completely aside and touched only once, at the end, for final evaluation and reporting.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Known confounds—socioeconomic factors (SES), comorbidity burden, and baseline severity—are explicitly included as features in both the transformer and baseline models. This allows the regression analysis to statistically control for these confounds. Subgroup analysis (stratified evaluation by SES quintile, comorbidity count category, and baseline severity) is conducted post-hoc to detect residual confounding or effect modification; if predictions are significantly biased within a subgroup, the design does not support causal claims about the transformer's advantage in that subgroup. Unmeasured confounds (e.g., adherence, social support) are acknowledged and scoped: the experiment tests whether the transformer predicts *observed* disease progression better, not whether it captures true causal drivers. Site-based holdout check (10% of test set from a second facility) detects practice-variation confounding; large divergence in performance between sites suggests the model has learned site-specific artifacts rather than generalizable disease dynamics.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Regression for Chronic Disease Progression Prediction

### 1. Data Source and Outcome Definition

The experiment uses electronic health records from a single regional healthcare system spanning five years (January 2019–December 2023), comprising approximately 100,000 patient records. Each record contains demographics, structured clinical data (diagnoses, symptoms, medications, lab results), free-text clinical notes, and longitudinal follow-up outcomes.

Disease progression is defined as the annualized rate of change in a validated clinical severity index (constructed from standardized lab markers, functional status codes, hospitalization frequency, and specialist referral patterns) measured over a 12-month follow-up window from an index encounter. This continuous measure directly aligns with the stakeholder objective: forecasting progression informs treatment intensity and resource planning.

### 2. Data Splits with Temporal and Stratified Logic

To prevent information leakage and reflect real deployment scenarios, a temporal split is employed:
- **Training set**: Index encounters January 2019–December 2020 (n ≈ 45,000 patients)
- **Validation set**: Index encounters January 2021–June 2021 (n ≈ 20,000 patients)
- **Test set**: Index encounters July 2021–December 2021 (n ≈ 15,000 patients)

Within each split, stratification is applied by disease type and baseline severity to ensure stable distributions across folds. Additionally, 10% of the test set (n ≈ 1,500) is held aside from a second healthcare facility within the system to evaluate generalization across institutional practice variation—a critical check for confounding by site-specific care patterns.

Temporal ordering prevents the model from implicitly learning future information; stratification ensures that performance comparisons are not distorted by unbalanced outcome distributions in subgroups.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All preprocessing transformers (standardization, imputation, tokenization) are fit exclusively on the training set, then applied identically to validation and test sets. This mirrors production deployment and avoids data leakage.

Features are constructed in two categories:

**Unstructured**: Clinical note text from the prior 6–12 months is tokenized using a pretrained clinical BERT tokenizer and truncated to 512 tokens.

**Structured**: (1) Diagnosis and symptom codes (ICD-10) as one-hot vectors; (2) Demographics (age, sex, race/ethnicity, zip-code–derived socioeconomic status quintile); (3) Baseline health (comorbidity count, lab values, prior medication classes); (4) Temporal (time since last visit, visit frequency, missing-value indicators for labs); (5) Derived (12-month rolling statistics of lab values, interaction terms between comorbidities and baseline severity).

Continuous features are standardized post-imputation. Categorical features are one-hot encoded. Missing data for continuous features is imputed using the training-set median; missing indicators are retained as separate features to capture informative missingness.

### 4. Models and Baseline

**Proposed model**: A transformer-based sequence model processes clinical notes and timestamped structured events (diagnoses, labs, medications) via a pretrained clinical BERT encoder. The final encoded representation is concatenated with demographic and baseline features, then passed through a 2-layer feedforward network (256 → 128 → 1 neuron) with ReLU activations. The model is trained end-to-end with MSE loss and L2 regularization (λ = 0.001).

**Baseline**: Multivariable linear regression with elastic net regularization (α = 0.5, L1/L2 mix). The baseline receives the same structured feature set as the transformer (excluding raw clinical notes, which it cannot process), ensuring it is not deliberately handicapped. Both models are tuned with equal rigor: 5-fold stratified cross-validation on the training set, with hyperparameters selected to minimize the primary metric (RMSE).

This design ensures a fair head-to-head comparison: the transformer's advantage, if present, stems from its ability to extract information from unstructured text and learn nonlinear feature interactions, not from unequal tuning effort or feature access.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics and Validation Approach

**Primary metric**: Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) on the test set. RMSE is chosen because (1) the outcome is continuous, (2) it penalizes large errors, which is clinically significant—poor predictions lead to over- or under-treatment, and (3) it is directly interpretable to stakeholders in units of severity points. RMSE is reported at the whole-cohort level and stratified by baseline severity, disease type, and SES quintile to detect performance disparities.

**Secondary metrics**: 
- Mean Absolute Error (MAE) — clinically interpretable, robust to outliers.
- Calibration plots by prediction decile — ensures uncertainty is well-calibrated.
- Spearman correlation — rank-order agreement, robust to outliers.
- Sensitivity and specificity for rapid progression detection (top 10% of outcomes) — operationally relevant for triage.
- Subgroup RMSE/MAE/correlation by disease type, severity, SES, and age — flags disparities.

**Model selection**: Hyperparameters are tuned via 5-fold stratified cross-validation on the training set. The top 3 candidate configurations are evaluated on the validation set to select the final model; any decision thresholds (e.g., for rapid-progression detection) are set using the validation set. The test set is held completely separate and evaluated only once, at the end, to provide an unbiased final performance estimate.

### 6. Confound Control and Scope

Known confounds—socioeconomic status, comorbidity burden, and baseline disease severity—are explicitly included as features in both models, allowing regression to statistically adjust for them. Subgroup analyses post-hoc stratify performance by SES, comorbidity count, and severity; divergent performance within a subgroup indicates potential residual confounding that should be noted in the interpretation.

The site-based holdout check (10% of test set from a second facility) detects whether the model has learned site-specific practice artifacts rather than generalizable disease dynamics; large performance gaps between sites suggest confounding by institutional care patterns.

**Scope**: This experiment tests whether the transformer predicts *observed* disease progression more accurately than regression—a forecasting comparison, not a causal one. Unmeasured confounds (adherence, social support) may drive true disease progression but remain unobserved; the model's advantage is in capturing patterns in available data. Claims are appropriately scoped: the finding supports deployment if prediction accuracy is the operational objective, but does not establish causal mechanisms.
